data.title: Navitas Semiconductor: After Nvidia's Kiss, It's Now Fairly Valued
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

Navitas Semiconductor Corporation (NASDAQ:NVTS) is a small-cap power semiconductor firm, which "offers gallium nitride power integrated circuits, silicon carbide power devices, silicon system controllers, and digital isolators for power conversion and charging," according to Seeking Alpha's description. Since the beginning of this year, NVTS stock price first fell by 50% by late April, and then skyrocketed overnight, fueled by a partnership with Nvidia (NVDA). Right now, NVTS is up over 66% on a YTD basis:

I think this YTD price action - the overall strength in NVTS stock that we see over the past few trading sessions - has created a major discrepancy between the stock price and the firm's underlying fundamentals that should theoretically justify the move up, but they fail to do so just yet, in my opinion. I think NVTS's valuation has become too fragile to sustain in the medium term, but I admit that the deal with Nvidia might make Navitas close to being fairly valued. Hence, my "Hold" rating initiation today.

Why Do I Think So?

Navitas Semiconductor's Q1 revenue came in at just $14 million (-39.5% YoY), beating the consensus by only ~$13.2 thousand (or by 0.09%), while the bottom line - their negative EPS of -$0.06 - was in line with the Street's expectations for the period. The general slowdown in channel inventory in EV, solar, and across the industry pressed the top line materially as the management repeatedly mentioned "soft demand" and "inventory correction" throughout the whole earnings call commentary.

As expected, the sequential decline was due to seasonality and soft demand with associated remaining inventory correction. <...>

Moving on to guidance for the first quarter, we currently expect revenues in the range of $14 million to $15 million, reflecting continued softness and industry-wide inventory corrections in solar, EV, and industrial end markets.

Source: Navitas' Q1 earnings call, composed by the author.

The CEO said that the firm needs a quarter or two to see the overhang of silicon carbide inventory gone, then theoretically, the top-line growth should reignite, which I'd perceive lie something uncertain, out of Navitas' control. While Navitas does have a pipeline of ~$450 million worth of design wins, as the management noted itself, the vast majority of that pipeline should come next year, with some trickling in late 2025 and a bit in 2027, so I think it'd be reasonable to spread out this lucrative figure to 2-3 years at least.

On May 21st, 2025, the news broke that Nvidia is going to collaborate with Navitas, taking their GaN and SiC expertise to Nvidia's next-generation 800V HVDC architecture for powering future GPU platforms like Rubin Ultra. And that news hasn't been reflected in Navitas Semiconductor's forward revenues just yet, based on the recent quarterly revisions:

On the other hand, the lack of upward adjustments for the firm's top-line doesn't mean that once such adjustments come, the stock should keep rising - the last week's price action seems to account for prospective reversal in Navitas' business:

Also, Navitas's management consistently mentioned "significant revenue from new design wins" for FY2026 during the Q1 call, so they probably meant Nvidia's contribution to the top line as well.

Anyway, as we know, Rubin Ultra server racks don't come out until mid-2027, so I'd not expect any substantial revenues for Navitas as monetization will likely only begin to be accounted for around that timeframe. Now let's assume that the recent trend in reducing OPEX-to-sales continues and the firm's backlog triples in size, which would effectively lead to a breakeven on a non-GAAP basis by FY2026, and then a doubling of the EPS figure in FY2027. I think that this assumption would fully account for the new partnership with Nvidia.

Consensus Revenue My Revenue Assumption Consensus EPS My EPS Assumption
2025 $64.02 $96.03 -$0.19 -$0.17
2026 $96.42 $289.26 -$0.1 $0.1
2027 $129.82 $389.46 -$0.04 $0.2
2028 NA $467.352 NA $0.4

Source: The author's calculations.

Considering my EPS projection for FY2026 and FY2027, the stock is currently trading at 61.7x and 31x forward price-to-earnings multiples, respectively. The FY2027 P/E multiple would be down to 15.4x if NVTS manages to double its EPS somehow. Money-making firms' P/Es from this industry are ~54x (TTM), on average, based on Damodaran's table, so NVTS would be only slightly overvalued compared to peers in this case. Looking 2 years ahead (at the FY2027 prospective EPS), which is hard to predict right now, but when following my assumptions, the stock would become truly undervalued.

The Bottom Line

I should note that initially, I wanted to rate Navitas Semiconductor stock as a "Sell," but then I realized that the newly announced deal with Nvidia may indeed add a lot of value - the backlog as of Q1 (at $450 million) was giving too low of a comparison base for their future top line figure, so it can easily double in the next 2-3 years from now as Nvidia prepares its Rubin Ultra server racks shipments. Based on my math and assumptions, which may be quite arguable in nature for some investors, the stock is close to being fairly valued now, when we look out 2-3 years from now.

Anyway, the lack of earnings visibility and the current challenges on EV and other end markets stop me from issuing a "Buy" - NVTS is a "Hold" for now.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Walmart: Some Ways We Could Profit Between Earnings
data.description:
Article Thesis

Walmart (NYSE:WMT) is an intriguing case. On one side, we have a relatively clear overvaluation, for example with a PEG ratio of 4.89, compared to 3.63 five-years average. More about valuation later.

On the other side, there is a curious bullishness of Wall St. analysts:

We can see a much more balanced view from SA analysts, tilted towards bearishness:

Although Wall St. analysts are many times driven by other things than the fair value of a stock, and many times they lag the stock movements, the problem is that they can influence the short-term price. Long-term, stocks almost always find their ways towards their fair value. Of course, a fair value is just an approximation, but I would say that there is not a high uncertainty for a business like Walmart.

It is, therefore, a difficult decision to short the stock when Wall St. analysts are bullish and very bullish. The potential profit from a short sale is also not very appealing (more about valuation later). But if we look at the options market, we can instantly find a plethora of…options, for example short-term strategies used between (or before) earnings, which can eventually be transformed into other longer-term strategies.

Business Analysis and Economic Moat

Walmart is the biggest retailer in the U.S., but they actually have three business segments:

Walmart U.S.: with stores in all states and e-commerce, this segment represents 69% of total sales.

This segment has an economic moat based on cost benefits. With its very large scale, Walmart has negotiating power, and a large distribution network. Normally I would say this is a strong economic moat, but this is eroding today due to the tariffs that Walmart “should eat”, even if more than two-thirds of what they sell in the U.S. is made, assembled or grown locally. The company expressed concerns about these tariffs during their last earnings call:

The immediate challenge is obviously navigating the impact of tariffs here in the U.S. Our mindset and approach haven't changed since our investor conference last month in Dallas. I want to thank President Trump and Secretary Bessent for the progress made recently. We're hopeful that it leads to a longer term agreement between the U.S. and China that would result in even lower tariffs. We will do our best to keep our prices as low as possible. But given the magnitude of the tariffs, even at the reduced levels announced this week, we aren't able to absorb all the pressure given the reality of narrow retail margins.

Walmart International: with stores in 18 countries and e-commerce, this segment represents 18% of total sales, China standing out with a double-digit growth last year.

This segment might have the same economic moat based on cost benefits, but with much smaller markets this is from the beginning only a narrow economic moat.

Sam’s Club: a membership-only U.S. club, representing 13% of total sales.

Like any network, this segment develops a moat based on…network effects: more offers can attract more customers, however these offers come with free shipping and discounts, so I would say only a narrow economic moat.

Overall, I would say somewhere between a narrow and a strong economic moat, trending down. This is also reflected in their ROI (Return On Investment) in excess of WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital):

However, please be aware that they are using Operating Income adding back Depreciation & Amortization. If we use NOPAT (Net Operating Profit After Tax), a more frequently used metric, this 15.5% would translate to about 11%, still above WACC, but not by much (more about WACC later), stressing again an economic moat, but not that strong.

Financial Analysis

In the last quarter, Walmart U.S. comp grew by 4.5%, while international sales grew by 7.8%, driven by China and Flipkart. Sam's U.S. comp grew by 6.7%, excluding fuel. eCommerce has a high contribution with global growth of 22%.

That’s why the expected growth rate of 4%-5% for the next two to three years is plausible:

Switching to profitability, the long-term trend is for higher growth than for sales:

There is nothing about this current period that makes us feel differently about anything we've previously said about our long-term financial framework to grow annual sales about 4% and operating income faster than sales.

This is driven by higher margin businesses like membership and advertising. eCommerce also crossed the profitability threshold, and I expect it to also help drive margins higher, since eCommerce is naturally a more scalable business segment.

However, there is, of course, the uncertainty brought by tariffs, a restoration of dramatically higher tariff levels could even jeopardize their ability to grow earnings YoY.

Finally, Walmart has a strong financial position, with an AA credit rating from Fitch, reflecting its dominant retail position and healthy leverage, and the same from S&P Global.

Fair Value

For a relative valuation, we can first look at Walmart’s five-year averages, because Walmart is not a cyclical stock, and today's multiples are about 40% above:

Then, compared to peers, we can observe about the same difference, between 30% and 50% above:

For an absolute valuation, I built a 10-year DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) with growth in perpetuity combined with EV/EBITDA exit. My assumptions are:

  • Revenue growth: starting from consensus for this year (see above section), I model 4.5% growth for 3 years, then 4% growth for another 3 years and finally 3.5% growth another 3 years, driven by eCommerce and emerging markets.
  • EBIT margin constant this year at 4.3% due to tariff pressures, then slightly growing towards 5.5% in 2034 driven by higher margin segments (see above section).
  • Growth in perpetuity: 3%, in line with global GDP growth.
  • Terminal EV/EBITDA: 13x, close to Walmarts’s 5Y average.
  • Tax rate: 22% (from ~23.5% in 2024 and declining).
  • CapEx: between 3% and 3.5% of sales, according to the company’s guidance; I use 3.5% for this year, then decreasing to 3% in 2028.
  • WACC = 7.6%: Cost of Equity = 8% (4.25% Risk-free rate, 5% equity-risk and 0.75 re-levered Beta), with ~90% weight, and after-tax Cost of Debt = 4%, with ~10% weight.

Fair Value is $68.2, about 45% below the current price (~$99.5), which is backed by the relative valuation. Or that means about 31% potential downside from the current price to Fair Value. We can further run a sensitivity analysis, by varying WACC and growth in perpetuity:

Fair Value ($)

WACC=7%

WACC=7.6%

WACC=8.2%

Growth=2%

69.6

64

59.2

Growth=3%

75.35

68.2

62.4

Growth=4%

84.9

74.8

67.2

We can observe that, even with more optimistic assumptions like WACC = 7% and growth in perpetuity = 4%, the stock still seems slightly overvalued with a Fair Value of $84.9.

Some Strategies Between Earnings

Setup

We have a company with narrow to strong economic moat, but with slow growth and about 31% potential downside to Fair Value. If we had 30% growth I could understand, but in this case, I see no justification for this valuation.

That does not mean that the stock cannot stay overvalued another year or two. For example, lower than 30% tariffs for China can be short-term positive for the stock. Or another sector rotation due to macro fears from sensitive to defensive industries can keep the stock higher short-term without any merit. Besides, Wall St. analysts are, curiously, mostly bullish. So, I am not interested in shorting the stock.

However, focusing on the power of options, we can see another setup: there are more than two and a half months until earnings (August 21), which means we can sell an option expiring just before earnings, on August 15, because options expiring before earnings lose value very quickly. At the same time, we can buy an option expiring after earnings (about one month after earnings, on September 19), with the idea to sell it together with the other option, because the next month option should normally gain some value just before earnings due to higher IV (Implied Volatility).

Strategy #1

One way to implement this setup is through a Calendar Spread (it does not matter very much if Call or Put options) and, since we are more or less bearish, we should place the strike price below the current price, for example at $92.5:

We can see that this strategy could have a remarkable leverage, with ROI about 160% in two and a half months with the stock exactly at $92.5 at expiration, because the short option will expire worthless, while the long one will have plenty of extrinsic value (especially just before earnings).

On the other side, what I don’t like is the very narrow range of profitability: between about $88.5 and $97.5. Outside that range, the long option will have more extrinsic value to lose. That means if the stock is going down very quickly, I should sell it when it hits $92.5 for a minor profit: the more distance to expiration, the lower the profit at $92.5, because the short 92.5 Put Aug 15 will still have extrinsic value.

But what to do if the stock will suddenly jump on its way down from $93 to, say, $87 (due to an unexpected event / announcement, like the reversal of China tariffs to 140%): we would probably need to close the position with a minor loss, despite the fact that I’m counting on the stock going down, since I find the stock overvalued!

Strategy #2

Let’s complicate the strategy just a bit, by choosing different strike prices, which gives us a Diagonal Spread (as usual, for investors not familiar with the strategy I do recommend them to read but to test it first in a demo account for about one year):

We observe that we can collect about 745 – 335 = $410 with the two Call options above.

The cost for this diagonal Call is a bit trickier…let’s suppose that, in a worst-case scenario, the stock will go to $125 on August 15:

  • The short 95 Call Aug 25 will be bought back for $3000 (125 – 95, multiplied by 100), maybe a few dollars more (let’s say $3020), because it’s usually better to buy it back just before expiration. Otherwise, since it is ITM (In-The-Money), we will be assigned (we’ll have short 100 shares), which is not a very big problem because we can close it immediately after assignment; however, if there is an ex-dividend on the same date (it can happen), we’ll need to pay the dividend ($24).
  • The long 105 Call Sep 19 will still have a bit of extrinsic value, and it will value more than $2000, depending on the IV at that time, and I’ve just said that IV should normally go higher before earnings (even for 90 days options); let’s suppose just $70 extrinsic value, so we could sell it for $2070.

Overall, at $125 just before August 25, we could close the position for about 2070 – 3020 = -$950. If we subtract the collected premium of $410, we get a $540 loss, and this is the cost of the strategy.

Some conclusions we can observe from the chart above:

  • Maximum profit with the stock exactly at $95 is about $450, for 450 / 540 = ~83% ROI, because the short 95 Call will expire worthless, while the long 105 Call will have enough extrinsic value. Lower than for the Calendar Spread, but still impressive in two and a half months.
  • The strategy gains at least that collected premium of $410 (or a ROI of 410 / 550 = ~75%) at any low price, since both Calls will be far OTM (Out-of-The-Money), even with the stock at $68 (Fair Value), or even at…zero, and this is what I like the most compared to the Calendar.
  • On the upside, we can see the break-even at ~$100.4 (a bit higher than the Calendar because we have a short at $95 instead of $92.5).
  • Above $100.4, the strategy will lose at an accelerated pace (just like the Calendar), but…in this case we will be even more bearish. Let’s suppose that Walmart goes to $110 and the strategy will lose about $350; now, we can switch to a more bearish strategy, like an ATM (At-The-Money) long Put Spread carefully chosen so our potential profit will be higher than this loss of $350, for an overall profit. With the stock at $125, we could switch an even more bearish strategy, like a long Put.

Strategy #3

And now, of course, we can combine the two strategies:

As we can see, we'll have higher ROI than for a Diagonal somewhere between $88 and $96, with that pronounced peak at $92.5 brought by the Calendar.

With the stock down, we still have a remarkable profit at any level, albeit a little lower than with the Diagonal, due to the loss brought by the Calendar.

With the stock up, the loss curve is about the same as with the Diagonal, since both the Diagonal and the Calendar erode at about the same rate.

Risks

I’ve just described in detail possible continuations if the strategy will bring us a loss in a first phase, on August 15, or even after two iterations, so we could still end with on overall profit. We might be able to open a second iteration with the stock at ~$110 (the proposed long Put Spread) for an overall profit without needing more funds, but a for a third iteration at ~$125 (the proposed long Put), or even between $110-$125 we will need for sure to add more funds for an overall profit. That’s why I target a smaller initial exposure, maximum ½ position (think that when we buy a stock, we can start with ½ position or less, and then we can average down).

Even more, for the moment I have only the Diagonal and I might also add the Calendar if the price keeps going up, in order to get a lower cost (the Diagonal makes a bit more than 80% of the combined cost, which I want to keep it under 1/2 of a normal position).

Of course, there is also the risk that the stock will go up, so against my position, and will stay up. However, to stay at such elevated levels (it will be significantly overvalued) for years, that means more than short-term market whims. Perhaps zero tariffs for China (an unlikely scenario). Or an even higher operating leverage (remember that I already modeled an increase in EBIT margin from 4.3% to 5.5%) but that would mean a main reorientation of the business model, for example towards mainly online (unlikely again). If there will be some signs of this scenario until August, I will still go with my proposed continuation strategies (or another bearish strategy), but I will target only a tie instead of an overall profit, trying to keep the overall exposure lower (at maximum ½ position).

Takeaway

Walmart has a narrow-to-strong economic moat, based on cost advantages especially from the Walmart U.S. segment, with its very large market share, but also on network effects from its Sam's Club segment.

Growth prospects are steady, with ~4% revenue growth expected, driven by eCommerce and emerging markets. Earnings are expected to grow at a higher pace, based on higher margins segments like membership, ads and, in the future, eCommerce. However, margins will be more or less pressured by tariffs this year.

Derived from a 10-year DCF model, and backed by comparisons with Walmart's 5-year averages or with peers, my Fair Value estimate is $68.2, about 31% potential downside from the current price (~$99.5).

Slightly bearish options strategies like calendar and diagonal spreads (or combinations) offer attractive risk/reward setups pre-earnings. We could sell an option expiring Aug 15, a few days before earnings, and buy an option expiring in September with the intention to also close it before earnings, when there should normally be a higher IV. If the stock will go up and the strategy will have a loss, we could eventually switch to more bearish longer-term strategies, since the stock will be even more overvalued (without major fundamental changes).

data.title: Couchbase Sees Capella Demand But Macro Concerns Continue
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Couchbase (NASDAQ:BASE) recently reported its FQ1 2026 financial results, beating both revenue and earnings estimates.

I previously analyzed BASE in February 2025 with a Hold outlook due to the potential for growth from agentic AI demand.

The company continues to see demand from customers for its Capella platform, but its topline revenue growth rate is slowing, operating losses remain substantial, and its capital allocation performance is shareholder dilutive.

So, my outlook remains a neutral Hold for the near term.

Couchbase’s Market And Approach

BASE’s primary business is in the Database-as-a-Service market via its NoSQL database configuration SaaS platform.

The company has been touting its Capella platform, which seeks to use Agentic AI technologies to assist organizations in automating various functions.

According to a 2024 market research report by MarketsAndMarkets, the global cloud database and DBaaS market was approximately $21 billion in 2023 and was forecasted to reach $57 billion by 2028.

If accomplished, this growth rate would be an impressive CAGR of 22% from 2024 to 2028.

The main drivers for this projected growth include strong growth in demand for greater capabilities and ease-of-use in cloud database systems that can operate in a variety of environments.

Additionally, SMBs will account for increased breadth of demand for these functions.

The chart below shows the forecasted growth trajectory by region through 2028 for the cloud database and DBaaS market:

Industry players include the following companies of varying sizes. Couchbase runs up against strong competition from a wide variety of industry participants with point solutions to bundled offerings:

  • IBM (IBM)
  • Oracle (ORCL)
  • Amazon (AMZN)
  • Google (GOOG)
  • Microsoft (MSFT)
  • SAP SE (SAP)
  • MongoDB (MDB)
  • EnterpriseDB
  • Redis Labs
  • MariaDB
  • Neo4j
  • MemSQL
  • DataStax
  • Others

The company sells its software via a standard 'land and expand' sales and marketing approach as well as through ecosystem partners such as resellers and integrators.

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT of the company's primary characteristics:

  • Strengths: Differentiated products, Capella Agentic AI system, Enterprise focus
  • Weaknesses: Lower brand awareness, smaller-sized player
  • Opportunities: AI enhancements, edge computing growth
  • Threats: Intense competition from much larger participants, need for continuous R&D spend to keep up with competition

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has risen 10% YoY from strong ARR growth. Operating income by quarter (red line) has remained negative but continues to make incremental improvement on lower SG&A costs.

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has risen in the last few quarters, although down one percentage point YoY. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have fallen due to an improved focus on strategic accounts. R&D expenses as a function of revenue (purple line) have been trending lower in recent quarters despite continued investment in its Capella platform.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have made some steady progress toward breakeven, but remain a long way from that important milestone.

(All data in the above charts is GAAP)

For balance sheet results, BASE ended the quarter with $147.2 million in cash, equivalents and short-term investments and no debt.

In the past 12 months, BASE’s stock price has fallen by about 18% vs. that of competitor MongoDB (MDB)’s similar drop of 18%, as the chart indicates below.

Below is a handy major financial and operating metrics table for Couchbase:

Metric

Amount

EV/Sales - FWD

EV/EBITDA - FWD

Seeking Alpha Quant Score

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

The company's Rule of 40 performance has produced improvement primarily due to a less negative operating margin offsetting a lower topline revenue growth rate:

Rule of 40 Performance (Unadjusted)

FQ3 2025

FQ1 2026

Revenue Growth %

19.3%

16.4%

Operating Margin

-37.2%

-28.8%

Total

-17.9%

-12.4%

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash used was $18.8 million, and capital expenditures were $3 million.

The company also paid a whopping $61.3 million in stock-based compensation in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was negative ($81.1 million).

The net dollar increase in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at $9.9 million.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was 29.3%, a very high number for a mature company.

Compared to MongoDB’s recent 24.6% result, Couchbase is paying out significantly higher SBC as a percentage of revenue while having a higher CapEx burden and still generating negative free cash flow.

Management hasn’t recently disclosed any future plans for capital allocation in relation to its shares, so investors are in the dark as to this aspect of its financial operations.

Why I’m Neutral On Couchbase

Couchbase has shown a reduced topline revenue growth rate along with improved, though still negative, operating margin.

This has been a common theme among operating loss-making software companies since the sharp increase in the cost of capital has penalized them heavily.

However, they have followed a similar trajectory of essentially sacrificing topline revenue growth for better operating results, putting at risk their ‘growth’ label and valuation in the bargain.

The company’s growth strategy entails investing in its Capella DBaaS platform, both on-platform features and integrations with ecosystem partners, positioning its offerings for higher-value propositions, and expanding its routes to market.

The graphic below illustrates the frequency of various typically negative keywords in the firm’s most recent conference call:

The chart shows the company continues to face macroeconomic challenges as clients take longer to make final purchase decisions, resulting in longer sales cycles.

Couchbase is also facing customer count decline as developers have moved from its starter pack promotion over to its free tier, although management says that really is expanding the top of the funnel.

Regarding valuation, the market is valuing BASE at a forward EV/Sales multiple of around 3.7x on an estimated NTM revenue growth rate of 13.1% against a median Meritech Capital SaaS Index implied ARR growth rate of approximately 14% (Source).

Also, the benchmark index indicates a recent median Net Dollar Retention Rate of 110% vs. Couchbase’s NRR of 114%.

The index of publicly held SaaS application software companies showed a forward EV/Revenue multiple median of around 5.3x on May 30, 2025, as the chart shows here:

So, by comparison, Couchbase is currently being valued by investors at a discount to the broader index as of May 30, 2025.

I’ve also compared Couchbase to competitor MongoDB, with their major metrics shown in the table here:

Metric

MongoDB

Couchbase

Variance

EV/Sales - FWD

5.9

-37.5%

EV/EBITDA - FWD

57.4

NM

--%

Net Income Margin

-6.4%
